join_order.description: Full parallel scan of lineitem (l_orderkey + l_quantity) → thread-local partial SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey → merge → filter sum > 300 → qualifying_orderkeys set (~10K entries) + qualifying_sums map

join_order.probe: orders_orderkey_hash index (10K targeted lookups, avoids 15M row full scan)
join_order.description: For each of the ~10K qualifying l_orderkeys, probe orders_orderkey_hash index to retrieve (o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_totalprice, o_orderdate) in O(1) per lookup. Avoids 15M-row sequential orders scan.
TPC-H c_custkey is dense 1-based. Direct array index at row (o_custkey - 1) into c_custkey.bin and c_name.bin. No hash table needed. Late materialization: load c_name only for ~10K qualifying rows.
SUM(l_quantity) per qualifying o_orderkey is already available from the subquery phase qualifying_sums map. O(1) lookup. No second lineitem scan needed.

logical_plan.subquery_detail: Decorrelate IN (SELECT l_orderkey FROM lineitem GROUP BY l_orderkey HAVING SUM(l_quantity) > 300) into a single parallel full scan of lineitem producing qualifying_orderkeys set and qualifying_sums map. Reuse SUM values in main query GROUP BY output — eliminates a second lineitem scan entirely.
logical_plan.aggregation_detail: GROUP BY (c_name, c_custkey, o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice) produces ~10K groups (one per qualifying order). Use compact open-addressing hash map pre-sized to 16K. Final SUM(l_quantity) per group retrieved from qualifying_sums map built in subquery phase.

filter_pushdown.predicate: SUM(l_quantity) > 300 per l_orderkey (HAVING after GROUP BY)
filter_pushdown.note: ~10,000 qualifying orderkeys out of ~15M distinct orderkeys. Applied after aggregation.
~10,000 qualifying orders out of 15M. Enforced via index lookup, not scan-time filtering.

data_structures.subquery_partial_agg: thread_local open_addressing hash_map<int32_t, double>(pre_sized ~2M per thread for l_orderkey domain) — 32 thread-local maps, merged sequentially after parallel phase
data_structures.qualifying_orderkeys: compact open_addressing hash_set<int32_t>(pre_sized 16384) — ~10K entries, fits entirely in L3 cache
data_structures.qualifying_sums: compact open_addressing hash_map<int32_t, double>(pre_sized 16384) — same ~10K entries as above, carries SUM(l_quantity) per qualifying orderkey for main-query output reuse
data_structures.orders_result_buffer: flat SOA vector of ~10K structs {int32_t o_orderkey, int32_t o_custkey, double o_totalprice, int32_t o_orderdate} — sequential allocation via arena
data_structures.customer_data: mmap c_custkey.bin + c_name.bin, direct row offset access at (o_custkey - 1), no hash table
data_structures.result_rows: flat vector<ResultRow> of ~10K entries: {char c_name[26], int32_t c_custkey, int32_t o_orderkey, int32_t o_orderdate, double o_totalprice, double sum_qty}
data_structures.topk: min_heap (std::priority_queue, size 100) ordered by (o_totalprice DESC, o_orderdate ASC) — O(10K × log(100)) ≈ trivial

parallelism.morsel_sizing_rationale: 44MB L3 / 32 threads / 2 columns (l_orderkey 4B + l_quantity 8B = 12B/row) ≈ 572K rows; rounded to 524K (power of 2) for alignment

parallel_phases.description: 32 threads each scan a morsel of l_orderkey.bin + l_quantity.bin. Each thread maintains a thread-local open-addressing hash map accumulating partial SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey. Threads work on non-overlapping morsels of the 60M-row lineitem.
parallel_phases.synchronization: thread_local_maps — no lock contention during scan. Sequential merge after barrier.

parallel_phases.description: Single-threaded sequential merge of 32 thread-local maps into global map. With ~15M distinct orderkeys per thread map (worst case) but typical ~10M unique keys, merge is dominated by the ~10K qualifying entries. Emit qualifying_orderkeys set and qualifying_sums map.
10K index probes into orders_orderkey_hash → customer direct lookup → top-100 heap. Trivially fast (~10K ops), single-threaded.

index_usage.purpose: Targeted lookup of order details (o_custkey, o_totalprice, o_orderdate) for each of the ~10K qualifying o_orderkeys. Replaces 15M-row sequential orders scan with 10K × O(1) hash probes. Layout: uint32_t capacity=33554432 then capacity × {int32_t key, uint32_t pos} (8B/bucket). Lookup: bucket = ((uint64_t)(uint32_t)key * 0x9E3779B97F4A7C15ULL >> 32) & (capacity-1); linear probe. Returns row index pos into orders column files.

NOT USED — SUM(l_quantity) per qualifying orderkey is already materialized in qualifying_sums during the subquery phase. Using this index would require re-reading l_quantity.bin for 10K × ~4 rows = 40K rows, adding unnecessary I/O and random access overhead.

physical_plan.scan_strategy: single_pass_fused for lineitem subquery phase; index_driven for orders and customer phases

lineitem_phase1.strategy: single_pass_fused — co-scan both column files in parallel morsels, fuse scan+accumulate into single loop per morsel

columns: o_orderdate.bin (read by row index from hash index lookup)
orders_phase2.strategy: index_driven — orders_orderkey_hash returns row positions, then random-access read of column files at those positions
orders_phase2.access: random_access_with_prefetch — 10K lookups, small enough to stay in L3 cache

customer_phase3.strategy: direct_array_index — c_custkey is 1-based dense integer; row = o_custkey - 1; read 26 bytes at offset row*26
customer_phase3.access: late_materialization — c_name loaded only for ~10K qualifying rows

physical_plan.memory_pattern: streaming_sequential for lineitem scan (phase 1); random_access_with_prefetch for orders/customer lookups (phases 2-3, tiny working set fits in L3)

phase_breakdown.description: 60M rows × 12 bytes = 720MB scanned across 32 threads. Effective per-thread: 720MB/32 = 22.5MB. With mmap sequential access and hardware prefetch, 32-thread parallel scan targets ~150ms.
Merge 32 thread-local hash maps. ~15M keys total across all threads; only ~10K survivors. Memory-bound on small working set.
10K hash probes into orders_orderkey_hash (mmap). Index file ~402MB+240MB; relevant buckets likely hot in OS page cache after lineitem scan.
10K direct array accesses to c_name.bin + result struct population.
Min-heap of 100 over 10K rows: O(10K × log 100) ≈ trivial.

estimated_cost.target_comparison: DuckDB 175ms / Umbra 180ms — plan designed to match or beat via: (1) 32-thread parallel lineitem scan, (2) index-driven orders lookup (avoids 15M-row scan), (3) zero second lineitem pass by reusing qualifying_sums
